# Questions in Livestream 171

Live Stream #171: A Census of Every Conjecture

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:26</a> | Why is older scholarship treated cautiously while church fathers are still quoted? | He says both older scholarship and church fathers can be useful, but both must be tested rather than accepted just because they are old. | He explains that some traditions quote the church fathers more heavily than others, but academic use still has to be critical. His example is Justin Martyr, who preserves useful material but also reads later traditions and first-century concerns back into biblical passages in ways that miss their original context. The point is that church fathers and old commentaries should both be weighed, not given a free pass because they are traditional or ancient. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=1141" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:01</a> | Is conflation a problem of wanting to compress teachings? | He says it can be, but compression is not the only cause; passive or lazy reading can also create conflation. | He answers the follow-up by saying compressed teaching can contribute to conflation, but it is not the whole explanation. Sometimes people read passively and merge details because they are not paying close attention. Older readers may have needed to hold more material in memory, while modern readers are especially susceptible to passive reading, so conflation can come from both compression and careless reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=1229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:29</a> | Why is the three-deben weight in your book shaped like a rabbit, and are all deben weights animal-shaped? | No. He says weights came in many forms, and animal shapes helped identify calibrated weights as weights rather than ordinary stones. | He says the rabbit-shaped three-deben weight was chosen partly because it was appealing, not because all deben weights were rabbits or even animals. Weights could look like stones or like different animals such as fish or turtles. Animal forms made calibrated market weights easier to recognize when packing up, and many also carried marks such as strokes indicating the amount of weight. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=1404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:24</a> | Will animal-shaped deben weights be available for purchase on the tour? | He says he wishes they were, but he has never seen them for sale in Egypt. | He treats the suggestion as an appealing souvenir idea and says animal-shaped deben weights would be more interesting than many tourist items. However, he says he has never seen one available for sale in Egypt, so the answer is effectively no unless such reproductions become available. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=1501" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:01</a> | Could the Merneptah Stele be referring to Israelites in the Sinai? | He says no, because the poetic geography and campaign setting point to Israelites in Canaan. | He argues that the stele's poetic section moves through categories and geography: major powers, coastal cities, wandering groups in Canaan, and then places tied to the campaign route. Merneptah would have taken the northern road through the fortress line toward Gaza and Gezer, not a route deep into Sinai. On that reading, the Israelites in the text are encountered in Canaan, likely along the Shephelah valley, rather than during wilderness wandering. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=1724" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:44</a> | Why would Pharaoh want Hebrew boys killed if he still needed labor? | He ties it to fear of a large population allying with Egypt's enemies, especially in the Hittite war context. | He connects Exodus 1:16 back to Exodus 1:10, where the fear is that Israel could multiply, join enemies in wartime, and leave the land. In his historical reconstruction, Egypt had enough labor for mud-brick work but feared a growing male population becoming a military ally of the Hittites during a long and unstable conflict. The policy is therefore framed as population control for security, not as a rational labor policy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=2107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:07</a> | Are humans a dichotomy or a trichotomy? | He says the Bible leaves room for both readings and does not settle the issue as cleanly as some traditions want. | He defines the issue as whether humans are body and soul or body, soul, and spirit, then says the biblical data are not hard and fast. Dichotomy has the appeal of simplicity, while trichotomy can make sense if spirit and soul are understood as distinct aspects in an internal community of intelligence. He refuses to choose dogmatically because poetic texts and other passages leave room for either model. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=2360" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:20</a> | What is the purpose of the second death in Revelation 20:12-15? | He says it is not annihilation, but final separation from life in Christ, while still involving resurrection to judgment. | He first distinguishes modern assumptions about death as nonexistence from an ancient Near Eastern and biblical idea of death as separation of body and soul. By analogy, the second death is a further separation: separation from God and from Christ, who is life. He links this to resurrection to judgment, arguing that the condemned still receive bodies but are cut off from life in Christ, so the second death is not annihilationism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=2980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:40</a> | Can you give background on Numbers 30 and vows? | He says the passage is about vows and household obligations, not simply controlling women, and the text gives different cases for fathers, husbands, widows, and divorced women. | He walks through the chapter as a set of household and financial responsibility cases. Men are bound by their vows, while a daughter in her father's house or a wife in her husband's house can have a vow annulled immediately by the person responsible for the household obligations. Widows and divorced women stand on their own because they control their own household obligations. He treats vows as contract-like obligations and says the issue is who bears the liability, not a simple rule for controlling women. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=3803" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:23</a> | What is your opinion on modern-day miracle claims, and have you ever witnessed any? | He says he has not personally witnessed a miracle, believes miracles can happen, but approaches modern claims with reserved skepticism and discernment. | He says he has seen strange things and believes the supernatural is real, but he has not personally witnessed a miracle. Because miracle claims can be misidentified or used in scams, he approaches them with skepticism while still allowing that miracles can occur. He especially distinguishes contexts where ordinary providential means such as doctors, surgery, and medicine are available from contexts where such means are absent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=4390" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:10</a> | Is the historicity of the Fall a hill to die on? | Yes, but he says the biblical claim should be kept nuanced and not expanded into unsupported claims such as no cell death or no entropy. | He says the Fall is essential to the plan of redemption because without a fall there is no need for Christ's atoning sacrifice. At the same time, he limits the claim to what Scripture actually requires: humanity violated sacred space through disobedience and came under condemnation. He rejects additions such as no plant cell death or no entropy before the Fall as going beyond the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=4662" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:42</a> | What current excavations might turn up significant Exodus-related data? | He points especially to work at Tell el-Retaba/Pithom and says more work south of the old estuary could help locate Pi-hahiroth or Baal-zephon. | He identifies Tell el-Retaba, which he associates with Pithom, as the major current project because a Polish-Czech team is doing rescue archaeology around a road project. He says finds such as more storage silos, the Temple of Atum connected with the name Pithom, parts of the fortress, and more dwellings would be significant. He also notes that wider work in the region could clarify Exodus geography, but his concrete example is the ongoing Retaba work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=5214" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:54</a> | What is your take on the bridegroom of blood in Exodus 4? | He reads the episode in its immediate context of Moses, Zipporah, circumcision, and covenant obligation, while stressing the passage is brief and difficult. | He reads Exodus 4:24-26 as a covenant failure immediately before Moses returns to Egypt. Egyptians and Israelites practiced circumcision, but Midianites did not, so Moses had apparently raised his sons like Midianites and neglected the Abrahamic covenant. Zipporah circumcises the son when Moses does not, saving Moses while objecting that the burden fell on her. The "bridegroom of blood" language is tied to that circumcision and Moses' failure to act. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=5641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:01</a> | Why do you not use the Diatessaron much? | He says he uses Kurt Aland's Synopsis of the Four Gospels instead because it places the Gospel parallels side by side and is more useful for his work. | He describes the Diatessaron as Tatian's harmonization of the four Gospels, but says he prefers Kurt Aland's Synopsis because it lays parallel Gospel passages side by side in Greek with translation. For his work, that arrangement is more useful than a harmonized retelling because it lets him compare wording and parallels directly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=6012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:12</a> | Is the harrowing of hell biblical? | He says it depends on what is meant: Christ descending to Hades or Abraham's bosom is biblical, but Christ suffering in hell is not. | He distinguishes Sheol or Hades as the abode of the dead from hell as a place of suffering. In his view, Christ went to the Abraham's bosom side of Hades to announce that sin had been paid for and to bring the righteous to heaven, but Christ did not go to the torment side to suffer. He also says the Latin rendering of the creed as inferno adds a problematic nuance not present in the Greek Hades wording. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=6324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:24</a> | Is Revelation 9's fifth and sixth trumpet about the rise of Islam? | He says probably not; he reads the passage as angelic judgment imagery rather than a reference to Islam. | He reads the fallen star, abyss, locusts, scorpion imagery, Abaddon/Apollyon, and the Euphrates angels as imagery of evil angelic beings released under divine limits. The fifth trumpet releases tormenting beings from the abyss, and the sixth involves angels leading armies and killing a third of mankind. He rejects the Islam identification partly because the passage ends with idolatry language, while Islam is heretical in his view but not idolatrous in that sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=7443" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:04:03</a> | Is life beginning at conception faith-based or scientific? | He gives a scientific answer: life begins when the new genetic complement starts cell division and meets biological criteria for life. | He says the scientific definition is tied to the new genetic complement beginning cell division. When the zygote divides from one cell to two, then four, then eight, it is growing and reproducing at the cellular level. He frames that as meeting ordinary biological criteria for life rather than depending only on a faith claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=7599" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:39</a> | Would Jesus have been able to cast the first stone at the adulterous woman? | He says no, because Jesus was not a witness; he also notes the John 8 story is textually disputed and the procedure in the story is irregular. | He reads Deuteronomy 17 as requiring witnesses to initiate the execution, and Jesus was not one of the witnesses. He also flags several procedural problems in John 8: the man is absent, the accusers are testing Jesus, and the story itself is not original to John's earliest manuscript tradition. Even if the event happened, he says Jesus could not properly cast the first stone because he was not a witness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=7866" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:06</a> | What did Jesus write in the sand? | He says we do not know, and the story itself is not in the earliest manuscripts of John. | He says the text does not say what Jesus wrote, so even if the event is granted for the sake of argument, the content remains unknown. He emphasizes that the pericope is a later addition to John and is absent from the earliest manuscripts, so speculation about the writing should not be treated as a firm biblical conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=8229" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:09</a> | Did Jews take more than one wife in Jesus' time? | Yes. He says some did, though economics limited the practice and it was not necessarily normal or desirable. | He says Jewish law allowed a man to have as many wives as he could afford, but the economics made multiple wives uncommon. Each wife had property claims, so later marriages quickly reduced what could be offered. He describes polygamy as permitted but not endorsed, comparable to divorce, and says it could affect a man's standing in the community. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=8404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:20:04</a> | Did Solomon's wives get a bad bargain under those property rules? | He says royal wives were different because most were treaty wives, so the ordinary property-division rules did not work the same way. | He says Solomon's wives did get a bad bargain, but not because the ordinary economic rule for multiple wives applied to kings. Royal wives were often part of foreign treaties, and a king's marriages did not divide property the same way ordinary marriages did. In that setting, the women functioned as treaty wives rather than wives receiving the usual property portions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=8529" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:09</a> | What were the practicalities of chariots versus riding horseback, and did New Kingdom Egyptians ride horseback in war? | He says New Kingdom Egyptians used horses for chariots, not cavalry, because Egyptian horses were suited to pulling but not bareback riding. | He says Egyptian horses were desert-hardy but had weak backs and were unsuitable for riding. Hyksos-imported Syrian horses mixed with Egyptian horses produced animals that could pull chariots well while still not serving as cavalry mounts. New Kingdom Egyptians therefore used chariots as mobile platforms for archery, spears, and lances rather than using horseback riders in battle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=8778" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:18</a> | Why weren't women priests in ancient Israel, and does this tie to Egypt limiting female priests? | He says Egyptian priesthood varied by cult, while Israelite priesthood was limited to a very specific male Aaronide lineage and not simply to men in general. | He says Egyptian priesthood was cult-specific, with some female cults such as Hathor or Isis having many women priests, so Egypt is not a simple precedent for excluding women. In Israel, priesthood depended on a narrow tribal and family pedigree: male descendants of Aaron within Levi. He stresses that most male Levites were also ineligible, so the restriction is not merely "men may be priests" but a very specific lineage rule. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=9163" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:43</a> | Did the English use Deuteronomy 22:5 correctly against Joan of Arc? | No. He says the English misapplied ritual law and treated armor as clothing for political rather than theological reasons. | He says Deuteronomy 22:5 is ritual law and therefore was not rightly applied as modern civil law. He also argues that armor is not ordinary clothing and that women had worn armor in earlier situations, especially in defense contexts. In his view, Joan of Arc was politically targeted, with the verse used as a pretext rather than a sound theological argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=9446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:26</a> | Is sudden infant death syndrome the work of God or Satan? | He says he is not a physician and does not know the medical cause, but treats it as a consequence of the Fall rather than directly God's or Satan's work. | He begins by saying he is not a medical doctor and does not know what causes SIDS. He rejects the forced choice that it must be directly God's work or Satan's work, calling that a false binary. His theological framing is that death, including heartbreaking deaths like SIDS, is an indirect consequence of the Fall and humanity's separation from eternal life, not something he can assign to a direct divine or satanic act. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=9622" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:22</a> | What do you think of the idea that the soul is not real and neuroscience or materialism will explain consciousness? | He does not find strict materialism compelling, saying that not fully understanding the soul or consciousness does not make them unreal. | He allows that the soul may be a phenomenon we do not yet understand, but says lack of complete explanation does not make something unreal, comparing that to gravity and electromagnetic radiation. He argues that conscious experience, mental imagery, and the continuity of self are not easily reduced to chemicals and matter alone. He finds neuroscience useful but says strict materialism is too narrow as a total explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=9903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:03</a> | What is the soul; could it be a fractal of the source? | He gives a speculative answer, leaning instead toward the soul as something like an uncollapsed quantum waveform tied to consciousness. | He frames the answer cautiously as his own tendency rather than a settled claim. He suggests brains may function like massive quantum machines, with the soul as a continuing wave state in flux that collapses at death. The point in context is that consciousness remains difficult to reduce to ordinary matter and chemicals, so his speculation preserves the idea that the soul is real even if not fully measurable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=9967" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:07</a> | After Jesus frees people from Abraham's bosom, where do righteous people go when they die? | He says that after the crucifixion, the righteous in Christ go directly to heaven rather than to Abraham's bosom. | He describes pre-crucifixion Israelite cosmology as having the dead in Sheol or Hades, with the righteous in Abraham's bosom because they were not yet finally redeemed. Enoch and Elijah are exceptions because they did not die. After Christ's crucifixion, those in Abraham's bosom are sanctified through Christ's blood and brought to heaven, so post-crucifixion believers in Christ go directly to heaven when they die. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=10324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:04</a> | What happened to Prince Zannanza? | He says Zannanza was the Hittite prince sent to marry Ankhesenamun and was intercepted and killed in Canaan, likely by forces under Horemheb. | He identifies Zannanza as the Hittite prince sent after Ankhesenamun wrote to Suppiluliuma asking for a son to marry and become king of Egypt. Suppiluliuma delayed while verifying the request, which allowed Ay and Horemheb to learn of it. In his reconstruction, Ay sent Horemheb to intercept the prince, who was killed somewhere in Canaan along with his escort, while Ay took the throne. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=10594" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:34</a> | If the cunning serpent is an angelic being, why is it described by God as a natural serpent in Genesis 3:14-15? | He says the passage uses metaphor and unlike comparison; the serpent language does not require a merely natural snake. | He says Genesis 3 uses comparative and metaphorical language, not a claim that the being is merely an ordinary animal. The Hebrew term can refer broadly to reptile-like or shining imagery, and the speaking, intelligent serpent already exceeds a natural snake. He reads "cursed more than cattle" and "on your belly" as unlike comparison and casting-down imagery, compatible with an angelic or cherubic being. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=11343" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:03</a> | Did Adam and Eve not fully understand God's rule because of their lack of knowledge? | He says they did understand the command not to eat, but Eve added "touch" to the command, which shows the danger of going beyond God's word. | He rejects the idea that they lacked enough knowledge to understand the command, and he also resists language that treats spirit as automatically good and death as simply evil in a Gnostic-sounding way. His key point is that Eve knew not to eat but added "touch" when restating the command. That addition created danger, because handling the fruit without dying could appear to confirm that eating was also safe, so the lesson is not to add rules beyond God's word. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=11618" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:38</a> | Who are your favorite sci-fi or fantasy writers with good technological realism, and how is your own writing coming? | He mentions Clifford Simak, Frank Herbert, Paul Anderson, and some Isaac Asimov, and says he has two completed sci-fi novels with another still developing. | He says what attracts him is good world-building, and names Clifford Simak, Frank Herbert, Paul Anderson, and some Isaac Asimov as favorites despite disliking Asimov's atheism. On his own work, he says he has two science-fiction novels complete, a third idea still percolating, and that he writes quickly once the idea is ready. His frustration is less with writing than with proposals and selling a book to publishers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=11692" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:14:52</a> | Can we get your novels as audiobooks? | Not yet. He says the novels have not been published. | He says audiobook versions are not available because the novels are not published yet. If they are eventually published, he says he would try to get them published properly, but at this point they are still not available in print or audio form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=12620" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:30:20</a> | Could Barry Kemp's mapping project be converted into an atlas by working with the family or rights holder? | He says he does not think so, because he lacks the data and rights and it would be a very large project. | He says converting the mapping project into a printed atlas would be difficult because he does not have access to the underlying data and would need to secure the rights. He also says taking it on would commit him to a major project when he already has enough large projects. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=13000" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:40</a> | Why was pork hated or prohibited for Israelites to consume? | He says the exact Israelite reason is not fully known, but connects it to Egyptian influence and Israelite clean/unclean categories of normal and abnormal animals. | He says the precise Israelite reason is unknown, though Egyptians deprecated pig farming because it polluted waterways, and Israel seems to have picked up aversion to pig from Egyptian influence. For Israel's clean and unclean categories, he frames the issue as normal versus abnormal animals: a cow fits the expected chewing-cud and hoof pattern, while pigs and camels do not. He also notes continuity between Israelite eating habits in Egypt and later settlement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=13156" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:16</a> | Were tarot cards originally Bible cards used to teach people? | He says the older major cards were tied to biblical-story presentation before later divinatory reuse. | He describes the original tarot deck as a set of picture cards without number cards, used visually by monks to present biblical theology to largely nonliterate audiences. In his telling, the order moved from creation, the garden, Adam and Eve, the Fall, Exodus, law, David, Christ, and onward to Revelation. He says later divinatory use came when others adapted the cards for fortune-telling, turning an originally didactic tool into something else. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=13393" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:43:13</a> | Is the word "blood" in Genesis 4:10 singular or plural in the original Hebrew? | He says it is plural, and explains that Hebrew can use plural blood language for shed blood on the ground. | He says the Hebrew in Genesis 4:10 is plural and treats that as a meaningful Hebrew usage. In his explanation, singular blood refers to blood in the body, while plural blood refers to shed blood on the ground in a postmortem state. He applies that same distinction to other biblical blood imagery, including the Nile and apocalyptic language, where the point is not simply red liquid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=13702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:22</a> | Was the pork prohibition due to trichinosis or hygiene concerns? | He says no; he does not think trichinosis explains the ancient Near Eastern prohibition. | He says trichinosis was not a major ancient Near Eastern or European problem and therefore does not explain Israelite or Egyptian pork avoidance. He treats it as more associated with North America and with animals eating contaminated meat scraps. In the context of the earlier pork answer, his point is that the prohibition should not be reduced to a modern hygiene explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=14187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:27</a> | What would you recommend reading on Genesis 1-11 as temple ritual or sacred-space theology? | He recommends starting with John Walton, while saying the topic still has room for more work. | He recommends John Walton, especially work such as The Lost World of Genesis, as a good starting point for Genesis 1-11 and sacred-space themes. He says Genesis 1-11 deals with sacred and profane space, first priests, first apostasy, and related sanctuary ideas. He adds that Walton will not cover everything he himself discusses, such as the ark as a tevah, and that the field still needs more work by people who know the material well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=14584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:03:04</a> | How long has the manger scene been inaccurate? | He says the Magi-at-the-manger conflation goes back very early in Christian tradition, while carved display scenes are much more recent. | He says the conflation of the Magi appearing at the manger is very old, already visible in Justin Martyr and therefore within roughly the first centuries of Christianity. The carved creche or display form is much later, which he estimates as beginning around the seventeenth century and being only a few hundred years old. The underlying problem, in his view, is that people do not read the text carefully. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=14742" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:42</a> | How did rich Roman aristocratic polytheists accept Jesus, and did Roman women accept Jesus more than Roman men? | He says many aristocrats resisted Christianity for economic and guild-related reasons, while women and slaves had fewer ties to those systems and often accepted it more readily. | He says Roman aristocrats were economically tied to guilds, patrons, and industry networks that were bound up with devotional cults, so many had pragmatic reasons to remain polytheistic. Roman women controlled household finances in some ways but did not depend on guild relationships in the same way, and slaves had even less economic stake in those systems. That is why he says Christianity spread more readily among women and slaves than among aristocratic men. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/IArgOM3IUb4?t=15355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:15:55</a> | What is the correct way to use "eh"? | He describes "eh" as a Canadian filler word, tag, and language softener, usually placed at the end of a sentence but sometimes used during pauses. | He says the Canadian "eh" is primarily a filler word and softener. It is often used at the end of a sentence to invite agreement, similar to "you hear me?" or Japanese "ne," but it can also fill pauses inside a sentence while the speaker continues a thought. He says it softens what is being said and notes that he does not use it much himself, though his father does. |
